title: Many-Body Non-Hermitian Physics in the Generalized Brillouin Zone
authors: Chaoze Lu, Chuanshu Xu, Zhenghao Yang, Xiancong Lu
abstract: The breakdown of conventional bulk-boundary correspondence (BBC) in   non-Hermitian system can be resolved by the generalized Brillouin   zone (GBZ) theory. However, extending the GBZ theory to interacting   many-body systems remains an open problem. Here, we consider an   interacting non-Hermitian model characterized by a circular GBZ. We   show that, based on a GBZ transformation, a quasi-reciprocal   many-body Hamiltonian can be constructed which, under periodic   boundary conditions (PBC), captures the physics of the original   non-Hermitian model under open boundary conditions (OBC). Using   exact diagonalization (ED), we determine the phase diagram for the   quasi-reciprocal many-body Hamiltonian by computing the Zak phase   and the structure factor of the charge-density-wave (CDW) phase. We   further investigate the entanglement properties and find that the   degeneracy of the low-lying entanglement spectrum characterizes each   phase in the phase diagram. These findings demonstrate that the   topological properties in interacting non-Hermitian system is encoded in   the entanglement spectrum of the quasi-reciprocal model. Our work   establishes a route to studying many-body non-Hermitian physics   within the GBZ formalism.
summary: This paper extends the Generalized Brillouin Zone theory to interacting non-Hermitian systems by constructing a quasi-reciprocal Hamiltonian. The authors demonstrate that the topological properties of this complex system are robustly characterized by analyzing the entanglement spectrum degeneracy of the derived quasi-reciprocal model. This establishes a crucial theoretical pathway for studying many-body non-Hermitian physics.
main_problem: The primary challenge addressed is extending the Generalized Brillouin Zone (GBZ) theory, which resolves bulk-boundary correspondence breakdown in non-Hermitian systems, to interacting many-body systems.
main_result: The authors successfully construct a quasi-reciprocal many-body Hamiltonian whose topological properties are encoded in the entanglement spectrum of the quasi-reciprocal model, providing a route to study interacting non-Hermitian physics.
method: The study employs a GBZ transformation to map the original non-Hermitian Hamiltonian (under OBC) to a quasi-reciprocal Hamiltonian (under PBC). Phase diagrams are analyzed using exact diagonalization, calculating the Zak phase and the entanglement spectrum degeneracy.
model_or_system: The model is an interacting non-Hermitian SSH-type model. The theory relies on constructing a quasi-reciprocal Hamiltonian ($	ilde{H}$) that mimics the physics of the original model ($H$) under different boundary conditions.
key_observables: Zak phase, structure factor of the charge-density-wave (CDW) phase ($S_{RL}(k)$), and the degeneracy of the low-lying entanglement spectrum (ES).
important_parameters: Interaction strength ($V$), hopping parameters ($t_1, t_2$), and the topological transition point ($t_{1c}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the applicability of the GBZ transformation to interacting systems and that the quasi-reciprocal Hamiltonian accurately captures the physics of the original model.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the mapping between the original and quasi-reciprocal models, show the phase diagram in the $t_1-V$ plane identifying different CDW and topological phases, and plot the dependence of the structure factor and ES degeneracy on system parameters.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the theoretical framework using the GBZ to derive the quasi-reciprocal Hamiltonian. It then applies this formalism to the interacting SSH model, using multiple diagnostics (CDW structure factor, Zak phase, ES) to map out the phase diagram and confirm the topological nature of the system.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, generalized theoretical tool (GBZ formalism) to analyze complex, interacting, non-Hermitian quantum systems, which is highly relevant for understanding open quantum systems and topological phases in condensed matter.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic diagram of the non-Hermitian interacting SSH model (a) and the corresponding quasi-reciprocal model (b). (c) The correspondence between βi in the circular gen- eralized Brillouin zone and crystal momentum ki in the con- ventional Brillouin zone.

FIG. 2. Phase diagram of the quasi-reciprocal SSH model in the t1 −V plane. The Top-CDW and Tri-CDW represent topological and trivial CDW phases, respectively. The red line shows the fitted CDW phase boundary given in the main text, while the black line with triangles denotes the phase boundary obtained from numerical extrapolation. The black line with squares indicates the phase-separation boundary ob- tained numerically for L = 20. The red dashed line (t1c ≈1.2 ) marks the topological phase boundary, and the blue dashed line (t1 = γ) indicates the non-Hermitian exceptional point. For t1 < γ, the energy spectrum becomes complex. Other parameter: t2 = 1 and γ = 2/3.

FIG. 3. (a) Charge-density-wave structure factor SRL(π) in the t1–V plane for a fixed lattice size L = 20. (b) SRL(π) as a function of the interaction V at t1 = 2 for various lattice sizes L. (c) Finite-size extrapolation of Vmax as a function of 1/L2, where Vmax denotes the point at which the derivative dSRL(π)/dV is maximized. The inset shows the derivative dSRL(π)/dV corresponding to the data in panel (b). (d) Zak phase γZ of ˜ H under PBC. The black line indicates the topo- logical phase boundary, with γZ = π in the topological region and γZ = 0 in the trivial region. Other parameters are t2 = 1 and γ = 2/3.

FIG. 4. Degeneracy structure D(n) of the entanglement spec- trum of the quasi-reciprocal Hamiltonian ˜ H in the t1–V plane. (a) D(4). (b) D(2). (c) log10[D(2) + 10−6], where 10−6 is in- troduced to avoid the divergence of log10(0). (d) D(5). The black and green lines represent the phase boundary in Fig. 2. Common parameters: L = 20, N = 10, t2 = 1, and γ = 2/3.
